title: L占폗ncien R占퐂ime; or The Good Old Rule
content:
혻혻혻혻Who has a thing to bring
혻혻혻혻For a gift to our lord the king,
혻혻혻혻Our king all kings above?
혻혻혻혻A young girl brought him love;
혻혻혻혻And he dowered her with shame,
혻혻혻혻With a sort of infamous fame,
혻혻혻혻And then with lonely years
혻혻혻혻Of penance and bitter tears:
혻혻혻혻Love is scarcely the thing
혻혻혻혻To bring as a gift for our king.

혻혻혻혻Who has a thing to bring
혻혻혻혻For a gift to our lord the king?
혻혻혻혻A statesman brought him planned
혻혻혻혻Justice for all the land;
혻혻혻혻And he in recompense got
혻혻혻혻Fierce struggle with brigue and plot,
혻혻혻혻Then a fall from lofty place
혻혻혻혻Into exile and disgrace
혻혻혻혻Justice is never the thing
혻혻혻혻To bring as a gift for our king.

혻혻혻혻Who has a thing to bring
혻혻혻혻For a gift to our lord the king?
혻혻혻혻A writer brought him truth;
혻혻혻혻And first he imprisoned the youth;
혻혻혻혻And then he bestowed a free pyre
혻혻혻혻That the works might have plenty of fire,
혻혻혻혻And also to cure the pain
혻혻혻혻Of the headache called thought in the brain:
혻혻혻혻Truth is a very bad thing
혻혻혻혻To bring as a gift for our king.

혻혻혻혻Who has a thing to bring
혻혻혻혻For a gift to our lord the king?
혻혻혻혻The people brought their sure
혻혻혻혻Loyalty fervid and pure;
혻혻혻혻And he gave them bountiful spoil
혻혻혻혻Of taxes and hunger and toil,
혻혻혻혻Ignorance, brutish plight,
혻혻혻혻And wholesale slaughter in fight:
혻혻혻혻Loyalty占퐏 quite the worst thing
혻혻혻혻To bring as a gift for our king.

혻혻혻혻Who has a thing to bring
혻혻혻혻For a gift to our lord the king?
혻혻혻혻A courtier brought to his feet
혻혻혻혻Servility graceful and sweet,
혻혻혻혻With an ever ready smile
혻혻혻혻And an ever supple guile;
혻혻혻혻And he got in reward the place
혻혻혻혻Of the statesman in disgrace:
혻혻혻혻Servility占퐏 always a thing
혻혻혻혻To bring as a gift for our king.

혻혻혻혻Who has a thing to bring
혻혻혻혻For a gift to our lord the king?
혻혻혻혻A soldier brought him war,
혻혻혻혻La gloire, la victoire,
혻혻혻혻Ravage and carnage and groans,
혻혻혻혻For the pious Te Deum tones;
혻혻혻혻And he got in return for himself
혻혻혻혻Rank and honours and pelf:
혻혻혻혻War is a very fine thing
혻혻혻혻To bring as a gift for our king.

혻혻혻혻Who has a thing to bring
혻혻혻혻For a gift to our lord the king?
혻혻혻혻A harlot brought him her flesh,
혻혻혻혻Her lusts, and the manifold mesh
혻혻혻혻Of her wiles intervolved with caprice;
혻혻혻혻And he gave her his realm to fleece,
혻혻혻혻To corrupt, to ruin, and gave
혻혻혻혻Himself for her toy and her slave:
혻혻혻혻Harlotry占퐏 just the thing
혻혻혻혻To bring as a gift for our king.

혻혻혻혻Who has a thing to bring
혻혻혻혻For a gift to our lord the king,
혻혻혻혻Our king who fears to die?
혻혻혻혻A priest brought him a lie,
혻혻혻혻The blackness of hell uprolled
혻혻혻혻In heaven占퐏 shining gold;
혻혻혻혻And he got as guerdon for that
혻혻혻혻A see and a cardinal占퐏 hat:
혻혻혻혻A lie is an excellent thing
혻혻혻혻To bring as a gift for our king.

혻혻혻혻Has any one yet a thing
혻혻혻혻For a gift to our lord the king?
혻혻혻혻The country gave him a tomb,
혻혻혻혻A magnificent sleeping-room;
혻혻혻혻And for this it obtained some rest,
혻혻혻혻Clear riddance of many a pest,
혻혻혻혻And a hope which it much enjoyed
혻혻혻혻That the throne would continue void:
혻혻혻혻A tomb is the very best thing
혻혻혻혻For a gift to our lord the king.
meaning:
This evocative piece by James Thomson -  (Bysshe Vanolis) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of James Thomson -  (Bysshe Vanolis)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.
